prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Penso a te ____ la cena da solo.' (mangiare)
explanation: 'Penso a te mangiando la cena da solo' means 'I think of you eating dinner on my own'. When you name an action that runs alongside the main one, Italian reaches for the gerund, the invariable -ando / -endo form. An -are verb takes -ando on its stem: mangiare gives mangi- plus -ando, so mangiando. The i of the stem stays (mangiando, not mangendo). Note that mentre would need a normal conjugated verb (mentre mangio); the bare gerund does the same job without it.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Ascolto la radio ____.' (lavorare)
explanation: 'Ascolto la radio lavorando.' means 'I listen to the radio while working.'. The gerund names the activity that accompanies another (here, listening to the radio while working). An -are verb forms its gerund in -ando: lavorare gives the stem lavor- plus -ando, so lavorando. The wrong ending -endo (lavorendo) belongs to the other two conjugations, not to -are.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Si è addormentato ____ la TV.' (guardare)

translation.reveal: guardare - to watch / to look at

explanation: 'Si è addormentato guardando la TV.' means 'He fell asleep watching TV.'. The gerund supplies the background action against which the main event happens (he fell asleep while watching TV). Build it from an -are verb by adding -ando to the stem: guardare gives guardando. Slipping to -endo (guardendo) crosses the conjugation line; -are keeps -ando.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Si impara ____ ogni giorno.' (studiare)
explanation: 'Si impara studiando ogni giorno.' means 'You learn by studying every day.'. Verbs whose infinitive ends in -iare (studiare, mangiare) keep the i of the stem when forming the gerund, because the ending -ando begins with a different vowel and does not swallow it: studiare gives studi- plus -ando, so studiando. Dropping the i (studando) or switching to -endo (studendo) are the two slips to avoid.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Risparmi tempo ____ online.' (comprare)
explanation: 'Risparmi tempo comprando online.' means 'You save time by buying online.'. The gerund expresses the means by which something is achieved (you save time by buying online). An -are verb builds it with -ando: comprare gives comprando. Note the trap here: comprendo would be read as a real but different word (the present 'I understand', from comprendere), so the -endo slip does not just look wrong, it lands on another verb entirely.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'È entrata in casa ____.' (cantare)
explanation: 'È entrata in casa cantando.' means 'She came into the house singing.'. The gerund describes the manner of the main action (she came in singing). For an -are verb the ending is -ando on the bare stem: cantare gives cantando. The rival ending -endo (cantendo) is for the -ere and -ire conjugations.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Ho incontrato Lia ____ il treno.' (prendere)
explanation: 'Ho incontrato Lia prendendo il treno.' means 'I met Lia while catching the train.'. The gerund sets the scene for the main event (I met Lia while catching the train). The -ere conjugation forms its gerund in -endo: prendere gives prend- plus -endo, so prendendo. Borrowing -ando from the first conjugation (prendando) is the cross-class slip the -ere class invites.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Ascolto musica ____ le email.' (scrivere)
explanation: 'Ascolto musica scrivendo le email.' means 'I listen to music while writing emails.'. The gerund names the activity going on alongside another (listening to music while writing emails). An -ere verb takes -endo: scrivere gives scrivendo. The -ando ending (scrivando) is the first-conjugation form and does not belong here.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Ho imparato molto ____ questo libro.' (leggere)
explanation: 'Ho imparato molto leggendo questo libro.' means 'I learnt a lot by reading this book.'. The gerund expresses the means (I learnt a lot by reading this book). Leggere is an -ere verb, so its gerund is -endo on the stem: legg- plus -endo, leggendo. The double g of the stem is kept; the ending is -endo, never -ando.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Ha imparato la lingua ____ poco e studiando molto.' (dormire)
explanation: 'Ha imparato la lingua dormendo poco e studiando molto' means 'He learnt the language by sleeping little and studying a lot'. The gerund expresses the means. Here is the key economy of the form: the -ire conjugation uses the very same ending as -ere, namely -endo. Dormire gives dorm- plus -endo, so dormendo. There is no -iendo (dormiendo) and no -ando; one ending, -endo, covers both the second and the third conjugation. (Studiando, alongside it, shows the -are ending for contrast.)

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Mi ha salutato ____.' (partire)
explanation: 'Mi ha salutato partendo.' means 'He said goodbye to me as he was leaving.'. The gerund gives the accompanying action (he greeted me as he was leaving). Partire is a plain -ire verb, and -ire forms its gerund in -endo, just like -ere: part- plus -endo, partendo. Writing -iendo (partiendo) inserts a vowel that is not there; the ending is simply -endo.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Si chiarisce tutto ____ il contesto.' (capire)
explanation: 'Si chiarisce tutto capendo il contesto.' means 'Everything becomes clear once you understand the context.'. Capire belongs to the group of -ire verbs that insert -isc- in the present (io capisco), but that insertion is a present-tense affair only. The gerund is built straight from the plain stem cap-: cap- plus -endo, capendo. Carrying the -isc- across (capiscendo) is the signature miss for this group; -iendo (capiendo) is the other slip. Neither belongs: the gerund of an -ire verb is just stem plus -endo.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Ho guadagnato tempo ____ il lavoro in anticipo.' (finire)
explanation: 'Ho guadagnato tempo finendo il lavoro in anticipo' means 'I gained time by finishing the work early'. The gerund expresses the means. Finire takes -isc- in the present (io finisco), but the gerund ignores it: the form is built on the plain stem fin- plus -endo, so finendo. The -isc- you see in finisco does not travel into the gerund (not finiscendo), and there is no extra i (not finiendo).

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Ha risparmiato ____ il treno all'aereo.' (preferire)
explanation: 'Ha risparmiato preferendo il treno all'aereo' means 'She saved money by preferring the train to the plane'. The gerund gives the means. Preferire is another -isc- verb in the present (io preferisco), but the gerund is built on the bare stem prefer- plus -endo: preferendo. As with capire and finire, the present's -isc- does not appear in the gerund (not preferiscendo).

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Ascolto i podcast ____ le pulizie.' (fare)
explanation: 'Ascolto i podcast facendo le pulizie.' means 'I listen to podcasts while doing the cleaning.'. A handful of verbs build the gerund not on their short modern infinitive but on a longer underlying stem, the stem expansion that also drives their imperfect. Fare expands to fac-, exactly the stem of the imperfect facevo, and adds -endo: facendo. If you know facevo, you already know facendo. Building from the contracted infinitive (farendo) is the slip this group invites.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Ha rovinato la sorpresa ____ tutto.' (dire)
explanation: 'Ha rovinato la sorpresa dicendo tutto.' means 'He ruined the surprise by telling everyone.'. Dire restores the longer stem dic- before the gerund ending, the same stem expansion you meet in the imperfect dicevo: dic- plus -endo gives dicendo. The teaching point is the parallel: dicevo and dicendo share the stem dic-. Building on the short infinitive (direndo) misses the expansion.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Si è sentito male ____ troppo caffè.' (bere)
explanation: 'Si è sentito male bevendo troppo caffè.' means 'He felt ill from drinking too much coffee.'. Bere hides a longer stem bev-, the one you see in the imperfect bevevo, and the gerund is built on it: bev- plus -endo, bevendo. The same expansion, the same stem. Building on the short infinitive bere (berendo) is the miss; the gerund needs the bev- that the modern infinitive has worn away.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Ha imparato l'inglese ____ canzoni.' (tradurre)
explanation: 'Ha imparato l'inglese traducendo canzoni.' means 'She learnt English by translating songs.'. Verbs in -urre (tradurre, condurre, produrre) expand to a -uc- stem, the stem of the imperfect traducevo: traduc- plus -endo gives traducendo. The contracted infinitive tradurre conceals the c; the gerund brings it back, exactly as the imperfect does.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: 'Ha aperto la lezione ____ una domanda.' (porre)
explanation: 'Ha aperto la lezione ponendo una domanda' means 'He opened the lesson by asking a question'. Verbs in -orre (porre, comporre, proporre) expand to a -on- stem, the one in the imperfect ponevo: pon- plus -endo gives ponendo. The contracted -rre of the infinitive is not the stem; the gerund uses the fuller pon-, matching the imperfect.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: '____ in ritardo, ha perso il treno.' (essere)
explanation: 'Essendo in ritardo, ha perso il treno.' means 'Being late, he missed the train.'. Essere is grouped with the irregulars for company, but it is actually regular in the gerund: it builds on its own stem ess- plus -endo, giving essendo. This is also the form that opens the compound gerund (essendo andato) and the essere half of the progressive, so it is worth knowing solidly.

prompt: Forma il gerundio: '____ poco tempo, ha risposto in fretta.' (avere)
explanation: 'Avendo poco tempo, ha risposto in fretta.' means 'Having little time, she answered in a hurry.'. Avere, despite its irregular present (io ho), forms a perfectly regular gerund on the stem av- plus -endo: avendo. The present's ho/hai/ha shapes do not reach the gerund. Avendo is also the avere half of the compound gerund (avendo finito), so it is high-value.

prompt: Completa al presente progressivo: 'Non posso uscire, ____ un articolo.' (leggere, io)

markpoints.label: sto (stare for the person and tense)

explanation: 'Non posso uscire, sto leggendo un articolo.' means 'I can't go out, I'm reading an article.'. To say an action is happening right now, Italian uses the progressive: stare conjugated for the subject plus the gerund. For io the stare form is sto, and leggere's gerund is leggendo, so sto leggendo. Two things have to be right: stare must be a finite form (sto), never the infinitive (sto leggere is the classic miss), and the second word must be the gerund, not the infinitive. The marker scores the stare form and the gerund separately.
examiner_note: Two markpoints per criterion 8, restored now that per-phrase match_at exists (Rev 17 §2). Markpoint A anchors the stare form (sto) with match_at "word", which is what makes this split safe: unanchored, 'sta' would match inside stai/stava/stanno. It catches the wrong person/tense and the essere-for-stare slip. Markpoint B anchors the gerund, catching the infinitive pairing ('sto leggere') as a gerund-formation miss. So a learner who writes 'sto leggere' hits A (stare right) and misses B (no gerund); one who writes the wrong stare form misses A and hits B.

prompt: Completa al presente progressivo: 'Ti sento appena, ____ ?' (dormire, tu)

markpoints.label: stai (stare for the person and tense)

explanation: 'Ti sento appena, stai dormendo?' means 'I can barely hear you, are you sleeping?'. The progressive is stare plus the gerund. The subject is tu, so stare is stai, and dormire's gerund is dormendo: stai dormendo. Watch the person on stare (stai, not sto) and keep the gerund pure -endo (not dormire, not dormiendo).
examiner_note: Two markpoints per criterion 8, restored now that per-phrase match_at exists (Rev 17 §2). Markpoint A anchors the stare form (stai) with match_at "word", which is what makes this split safe: unanchored, 'sta' would match inside stai/stava/stanno. It catches the wrong person/tense and the essere-for-stare slip. Markpoint B anchors the gerund, catching the infinitive pairing ('stai dormire') as a gerund-formation miss. So a learner who writes 'stai dormire' hits A (stare right) and misses B (no gerund); one who writes the wrong stare form misses A and hits B.

prompt: Completa al presente progressivo: 'Non disturbare il bambino, ____ adesso.' (mangiare, lui)

markpoints.label: sta (stare for the person and tense)

explanation: 'Non disturbare il bambino, sta mangiando adesso.' means 'Don't disturb the child, he's eating right now.'. Stare plus the gerund builds the progressive. For lui the stare form is sta (no accent: sta, not stà), and mangiare's gerund is mangiando, so sta mangiando. The two skills are the stare person and the gerund; the marker checks each.
examiner_note: Two markpoints per criterion 8, restored now that per-phrase match_at exists (Rev 17 §2). Markpoint A anchors the stare form (sta) with match_at "word", which is what makes this split safe: unanchored, 'sta' would match inside stai/stava/stanno. It catches the wrong person/tense and the essere-for-stare slip. Markpoint B anchors the gerund, catching the infinitive pairing ('sta mangiare') as a gerund-formation miss. So a learner who writes 'sta mangiare' hits A (stare right) and misses B (no gerund); one who writes the wrong stare form misses A and hits B.

prompt: Completa al presente progressivo: 'In questo momento ____ a un nuovo progetto.' (lavorare, noi)

markpoints.label: stiamo (stare for the person and tense)

explanation: 'In questo momento stiamo lavorando a un nuovo progetto.' means 'Right now we're working on a new project.'. 'In questo momento' (right now) calls for the progressive: stare plus the gerund. For noi the stare form is stiamo, and lavorare gives lavorando, so stiamo lavorando. A common confusion is to use the auxiliary essere (siamo lavorando), but the progressive is built with stare, not essere.
examiner_note: Two markpoints per criterion 8, restored now that per-phrase match_at exists (Rev 17 §2). Markpoint A anchors the stare form (stiamo) with match_at "word", which is what makes this split safe: unanchored, 'sta' would match inside stai/stava/stanno. It catches the wrong person/tense and the essere-for-stare slip. Markpoint B anchors the gerund, catching the infinitive pairing ('stiamo lavorare') as a gerund-formation miss. So a learner who writes 'stiamo lavorare' hits A (stare right) and misses B (no gerund); one who writes the wrong stare form misses A and hits B.

prompt: Completa al presente progressivo: 'Ragazzi, ____ troppo lentamente!' (scrivere, voi)

markpoints.label: state (stare for the person and tense)

explanation: 'Ragazzi, state scrivendo troppo lentamente!' means 'Lads, you're writing too slowly!'. The progressive is stare plus the gerund. The subject is voi, so stare is state, and scrivere's gerund is scrivendo: state scrivendo. Keep stare finite (not the infinitive) and use stare, not essere (siete scrivendo is the auxiliary slip).
examiner_note: Two markpoints per criterion 8, restored now that per-phrase match_at exists (Rev 17 §2). Markpoint A anchors the stare form (state) with match_at "word", which is what makes this split safe: unanchored, 'sta' would match inside stai/stava/stanno. It catches the wrong person/tense and the essere-for-stare slip. Markpoint B anchors the gerund, catching the infinitive pairing ('state scrivere') as a gerund-formation miss. So a learner who writes 'state scrivere' hits A (stare right) and misses B (no gerund); one who writes the wrong stare form misses A and hits B.

prompt: Completa al presente progressivo: 'I bambini ____ in giardino.' (giocare)

markpoints.label: stanno (stare for the person and tense)

explanation: 'I bambini stanno giocando in giardino.' means 'The children are playing in the garden.'. 'I bambini' (the children) is third-person plural, so stare is stanno, and giocare's gerund is giocando: stanno giocando. The subject is right there in the sentence, so map it to stanno (plural), not sta (singular). The gerund keeps the hard c of giocare (giocando).
examiner_note: Two markpoints per criterion 8, restored now that per-phrase match_at exists (Rev 17 §2). Markpoint A anchors the stare form (stanno) with match_at "word", which is what makes this split safe: unanchored, 'sta' would match inside stai/stava/stanno. It catches the wrong person/tense and the essere-for-stare slip. Markpoint B anchors the gerund, catching the infinitive pairing ('stanno giocare') as a gerund-formation miss. So a learner who writes 'stanno giocare' hits A (stare right) and misses B (no gerund); one who writes the wrong stare form misses A and hits B.

prompt: Completa al presente progressivo: 'Zitti! La nonna ____ un discorso.' (fare)

markpoints.label: sta (stare for the person and tense)

explanation: 'Zitti! La nonna sta facendo un discorso.' means 'Quiet! Grandma is making a speech.'. This progressive uses an irregular gerund. Stare for la nonna (3sg) is sta, and fare's gerund is the stem-expanded facendo (fac-, as in facevo), so sta facendo. The assembly is the same as for regular verbs; only the gerund is irregular. Building fare's gerund from the short infinitive (farendo) is the miss.
examiner_note: Two markpoints per criterion 8, restored now that per-phrase match_at exists (Rev 17 §2). Markpoint A anchors the stare form (sta) with match_at "word", which is what makes this split safe: unanchored, 'sta' would match inside stai/stava/stanno. It catches the wrong person/tense and the essere-for-stare slip. Markpoint B anchors the gerund, catching the infinitive pairing ('sta fare') as a gerund-formation miss. So a learner who writes 'sta fare' hits A (stare right) and misses B (no gerund); one who writes the wrong stare form misses A and hits B.

prompt: Completa al passato progressivo: 'Quando hai chiamato, ____ il giornale.' (leggere, io)

markpoints.label: stavo (stare for the person and tense)

explanation: 'Quando hai chiamato, stavo leggendo il giornale.' means 'When you called, I was reading the newspaper.'. The progressive works in any tense: put stare in the tense you need and keep the gerund unchanged. Here the action was ongoing in the past ('when you called, I was reading'), so stare goes into the imperfect, stavo, and the gerund stays leggendo: stavo leggendo. The gerund never changes with tense; only stare moves.
examiner_note: Two markpoints per criterion 8, restored now that per-phrase match_at exists (Rev 17 §2). Markpoint A anchors the stare form (stavo) with match_at "word", which is what makes this split safe: unanchored, 'sta' would match inside stai/stava/stanno. It catches the wrong person/tense and the essere-for-stare slip. Markpoint B anchors the gerund, catching the infinitive pairing ('stavo leggere') as a gerund-formation miss. So a learner who writes 'stavo leggere' hits A (stare right) and misses B (no gerund); one who writes the wrong stare form misses A and hits B.

prompt: Completa al passato progressivo: 'Non ha sentito il campanello perché ____.' (dormire, lui)

markpoints.label: stava (stare for the person and tense)

explanation: 'Non ha sentito il campanello perché stava dormendo.' means 'He didn't hear the doorbell because he was sleeping.'. An action in progress in the past takes the past progressive: stare in the imperfect plus the gerund. For lui that is stava, and dormire's gerund is dormendo, so stava dormendo ('he was sleeping'). Only stare carries the tense and person; the gerund is invariable.
examiner_note: Two markpoints per criterion 8, restored now that per-phrase match_at exists (Rev 17 §2). Markpoint A anchors the stare form (stava) with match_at "word", which is what makes this split safe: unanchored, 'sta' would match inside stai/stava/stanno. It catches the wrong person/tense and the essere-for-stare slip. Markpoint B anchors the gerund, catching the infinitive pairing ('stava dormire') as a gerund-formation miss. So a learner who writes 'stava dormire' hits A (stare right) and misses B (no gerund); one who writes the wrong stare form misses A and hits B.

prompt: Completa al progressivo futuro: 'Domani a quest'ora ____ ancora.' (lavorare, io)

markpoints.label: starò (stare for the person and tense)

explanation: 'Domani a quest'ora starò lavorando ancora.' means 'Tomorrow at this time I'll still be working.'. The progressive reaches into the future too: 'Domani a quest'ora' (tomorrow at this time) projects an action still in progress then. Stare goes into the future, starò, and the gerund stays lavorando: starò lavorando ('I will still be working'). As always, only stare changes tense; the accented starò is the future, distinct from the bare staro.
examiner_note: Two markpoints per criterion 8, restored now that per-phrase match_at exists (Rev 17 §2). Markpoint A anchors the stare form (starò) with match_at "word", which is what makes this split safe: unanchored, 'sta' would match inside stai/stava/stanno. It catches the wrong person/tense and the essere-for-stare slip. Markpoint B anchors the gerund, catching the infinitive pairing ('starò lavorare') as a gerund-formation miss. So a learner who writes 'starò lavorare' hits A (stare right) and misses B (no gerund); one who writes the wrong stare form misses A and hits B.

prompt: Forma il gerundio passato: '____ i compiti, è uscito a giocare.' (finire)
explanation: 'Avendo finito i compiti, è uscito a giocare.' means 'Having finished his homework, he went out to play.'. The compound gerund (gerundio passato) marks an action finished before the main one: 'having finished his homework, he went out'. It is built like the passato prossimo, avere or essere plus the past participle, but with the auxiliary in its gerund form. Finire takes avere here (it has a direct object, i compiti), so the auxiliary is avendo, and the participle is finito: avendo finito. With avere the participle does not agree with the subject.
examiner_note: Two markpoints per criterion 8: the auxiliary choice (avendo, not essendo) and the participle form. Cross-references the passato prossimo participle-form bucket (ire_ito). The auxiliary-choice skill is captured by the gerundio_passato bucket; the participle by the PP tree.

prompt: Forma il gerundio passato: 'Maria, ____ via presto, ha evitato il traffico.' (andare)
explanation: 'Maria, essendo andata via presto, ha evitato il traffico.' means 'Maria, having left early, avoided the traffic.'. The compound gerund of a motion verb uses essendo, the gerund of essere, plus the past participle, and because the auxiliary is essere the participle agrees with the subject. The subject is Maria (feminine singular), so the participle is andata: essendo andata ('having left early'). Using avere (avendo andato) is the wrong auxiliary, and leaving the participle masculine (essendo andato) misses the agreement.
examiner_note: Two markpoints: auxiliary (essendo, not avendo) on the gerundio_passato bucket, and the feminine-singular agreement on the PP agreement bucket. Subject Maria pins the gender; the cue gives only the verb.

prompt: Forma il gerundio passato: 'I ragazzi, ____ all'alba, sono arrivati per pranzo.' (partire)

markpoints.label: partiti (participle agrees with i ragazzi, m.pl)

explanation: 'I ragazzi, essendo partiti all'alba, sono arrivati per pranzo.' means 'The boys, having left at dawn, arrived in time for lunch.'. Partire is a motion verb, so its compound gerund is essendo plus the participle, with agreement on the participle. The subject is i ragazzi (masculine plural), so the participle is partiti: essendo partiti ('having left at dawn'). The auxiliary must be essere (essendo), not avere, and the participle must match the plural subject.
examiner_note: Masculine-plural agreement variant. Markpoint A: auxiliary; markpoint B: PP agreement (masculine plural).

prompt: Forma il gerundio passato: 'Lei, ____ un panino poco prima, non aveva fame.' (mangiare)
explanation: 'Lei, avendo mangiato un panino poco prima, non aveva fame' means 'She, having eaten a sandwich shortly before, was not hungry'. Even with a feminine subject (lei), the compound gerund of mangiare keeps the participle invariable, because mangiare takes avere and participles with avere do not agree with the subject. So the form is avendo mangiato, not 'essendo mangiata'. The trap is to agree the participle to the feminine subject (mangiata); with avere it stays mangiato.
examiner_note: Deliberate contrast with the essere items: the subject is feminine but the avere participle stays invariable (mangiato). Markpoint B rejects the over-agreement 'mangiata' and the gerund-for-participle slip 'mangiando'.

prompt: Esprimi il mezzo con un gerundio [it is by making mistakes that one learns]: '____ s'impara.' (sbagliare)

translation.reveal: sbagliare - to make a mistake / to be wrong

explanation: 'Sbagliando s'impara' means 'One learns by making mistakes' (a proverb). The bare gerund, without stare, works like an adverb on the main verb: here it answers 'by what means?'. The means gerund is the plain -ando / -endo form, so sbagliare gives sbagliando. Its unspoken subject is the same as the main clause's (the same 'one' both errs and learns), which is the rule that governs every adverbial gerund.

prompt: Esprimi il modo con un gerundio [running]: 'È entrato in classe ____.' (correre)
explanation: 'È entrato in classe correndo' means 'He came into the classroom running'. The gerund of manner describes HOW the main action happened. Correre is an -ere verb, so its gerund is correndo. The runner and the enterer are the same person, as the adverbial gerund always requires.

prompt: Esprimi il mezzo con un gerundio [by watching]: 'Ho imparato molte parole ____ i film italiani.' (guardare)

translation.reveal: guardare - to watch / to look at

explanation: 'Ho imparato molte parole guardando i film italiani' means 'I learnt many words by watching Italian films'. The means gerund says by what activity the learning happened. Guardare is an -are verb, so guardando. I did both the watching and the learning, so the same-subject rule is satisfied.

prompt: Esprimi il modo con un gerundio [smiling]: 'Ci ha salutati ____.' (sorridere)
explanation: 'Ci ha salutati sorridendo' means 'He greeted us smiling'. The gerund of manner paints how the greeting was done. Sorridere is an -ere verb, giving sorridendo. The smiler is the greeter, so the adverbial gerund is licensed.

prompt: Esprimi il mezzo con un gerundio [by swimming]: 'Si tiene in forma ____ ogni mattina.' (nuotare)
explanation: 'Si tiene in forma nuotando ogni mattina' means 'She keeps fit by swimming every morning'. The means gerund names the activity that achieves the result. Nuotare is an -are verb, so nuotando.

prompt: Esprimi il mezzo con un gerundio [by selling]: 'Si è pagato gli studi ____ gelati d'estate.' (vendere)
explanation: 'Si è pagato gli studi vendendo gelati d'estate' means 'He paid for his studies by selling ice creams in the summer'. The means gerund answers how he managed it. Vendere is an -ere verb, so vendendo.

prompt: Only one is correct standard Italian for 'While I was reading, the phone rang' (two different subjects, I / the phone). Which?
choices: Mentre leggevo, il telefono ha squillato.
Avendo letto, il telefono ha squillato.

markpoints.label: same-subject constraint: use a clause when subjects differ

explanation: The right answer is 'Mentre leggevo, il telefono ha squillato' ('While I was reading, the phone rang'). The adverbial gerund carries its own hidden subject, and that subject must be the SAME as the main clause's. Here the reader (I) and the ringer (the phone) are different, so a gerund ('Leggendo, il telefono ha squillato') would wrongly say the phone was reading. When the subjects differ, Italian keeps a full subordinate clause (mentre + imperfect), not a gerund.
examiner_note: MCQ index-scored; the markpoint keys off the chosen option's text. The distractors are the two gerund constructions (bare and compound) that violate the same-subject rule. This is the headline usage diagnostic that formation never touches.

prompt: Apri la subordinata di tempo con un gerundio [while coming home]: '____ a casa, ho incontrato un vecchio amico.' (tornare)

translation.reveal: tornare - to return / to come back

explanation: 'Tornando a casa, ho incontrato un vecchio amico' means 'While coming home, I met an old friend'. Here the gerund opens a subordinate of time, replacing 'mentre tornavo'. Tornare is an -are verb, so tornando. I did both the returning and the meeting, so the same-subject rule holds.

prompt: Apri la subordinata di causa con un gerundio [since it was already late]: '____ già tardi, abbiamo deciso di rientrare.' (essere)
explanation: 'Essendo già tardi, abbiamo deciso di rientrare' means 'It being already late, we decided to head back'. The gerund can open a subordinate of cause, standing in for 'siccome era tardi'. Essere's gerund is essendo. This causal gerund is common in slightly formal or written Italian.

prompt: Apri la subordinata di causa con un gerundio [not knowing what to say]: 'Non ____ cosa dire, è rimasto in silenzio.' (sapere)
explanation: 'Non sapendo cosa dire, è rimasto in silenzio' means 'Not knowing what to say, he stayed silent'. The causal gerund is negated simply by putting non in front. Sapere's gerund is regular on its own stem: sapendo (not sappiendo, which would borrow the present sappia-). Same subject throughout: he both did not know and stayed silent.

prompt: Apri la subordinata di condizione con un gerundio [if you wanted to]: '____, potresti restare a cena con noi.' (volere)
explanation: 'Volendo, potresti restare a cena con noi' means 'If you wanted, you could stay for dinner with us'. The gerund can carry a conditional sense, standing in for 'se volessi'. Volere's gerund is volendo. 'Volendo' on its own is a common idiom meaning 'if one wishes / it's possible'.

prompt: Completa la concessiva con un gerundio [although he was tired]: 'Pur ____ stanco, ha finito il lavoro.' (essere)
explanation: 'Pur essendo stanco, ha finito il lavoro' means 'Although he was tired, he finished the work'. Put pur (or pure) in front of a gerund and it turns concessive, meaning 'although / even though'. Essere's gerund is essendo. Without pur the same gerund would read as cause ('being tired'); pur flips it to concession.
examiner_note: The pur + gerund = 'although' construction is the C1 point. The cue supplies pur in the prompt (the trigger), so the learner produces only the gerund; criterion 13/26 compliant (the chip names the operand essere, not the concessive diagnostic).

prompt: Apri la subordinata di tempo con un gerundio [arriving late]: '____ in ritardo, ho perso l'inizio del film.' (arrivare)
explanation: 'Arrivando in ritardo, ho perso l'inizio del film' means 'Arriving late, I missed the start of the film'. The temporal gerund sets the time frame for the main event. Arrivare is an -are verb, so arrivando. I both arrived and missed, so the same subject runs through.

prompt: Apri la subordinata con un gerundio composto [having finished his homework]: '____ i compiti, è uscito a giocare.' (finire)

markpoints.label: compound gerund heading a subordinate of anteriority (function)

explanation: 'Avendo finito i compiti, è uscito a giocare' means 'Having finished his homework, he went out to play'. When the gerund's action comes BEFORE the main one, Italian uses the compound gerund (avendo / essendo + participle), not the simple gerund: finendo would wrongly make the two simultaneous. Producing avendo finito here evidences two things at once, so it is credited twice: the usage function (a gerund heading a subordinate of prior time/cause) and the compound form itself.
examiner_note: Rev 27 cross-credit / dual-citation: two markpoints at credit 0.5 with the same anchored phrase, one on the usage leaf (the function) and one on formation.gerundio_passato (the form). Item marks sum to 1; each bucket records a full hit. This is the genuine overlap the adverbial gerund has - with FORMATION, not with tense_choice (there is no progressive contrast here). must_not on the form markpoint rejects the simple gerund finendo, which is the anteriority error.

prompt: Only one is correct standard Italian for 'When I entered the room, the lights went out' (two different subjects, I / the lights). Which?
choices: Quando sono entrato nella stanza, le luci si sono spente.
Entrando nella stanza, le luci si sono spente.
Le luci si sono spente entrando nella stanza.
Essendo entrato nella stanza, le luci si sono spente.

markpoints.label: same-subject constraint: keep a clause when subjects differ

translation.reveal: entrare - to enter / to go in

explanation: The right answer is 'Quando sono entrato nella stanza, le luci si sono spente' ('When I entered the room, the lights went out'). The person entering (I) and the thing going out (the lights) are different, so a gerund would misattribute the entering to the lights. The same-subject rule forces a full clause (quando + verb) whenever the two subjects differ. Both the simple gerund (entrando) and the compound (essendo entrato) are wrong here for the same reason.
examiner_note: Same-subject constraint at the temporal_causal leaf, C1. Index-scored MCQ; distractors are the bare and compound gerunds plus a post-posed gerund, all violating the same-subject rule.

prompt: Which is the natural Italian for 'You learn a language by speaking it'?
choices: Si impara una lingua per parlarla.
Si impara una lingua che si parla.

markpoints.label: means expressed by the gerund, not by per + infinitive

explanation: The right answer is 'Si impara una lingua parlandola' ('You learn a language by speaking it'). English 'by ...-ing' for means maps onto the bare gerund, not onto per + infinitive (which expresses purpose, 'in order to', a different meaning). The clitic la attaches to the end of the gerund: parlandola. Choosing per parlarla would say you learn in order to speak it, which is not the point.
examiner_note: Contrasts the means gerund with the purpose construction per + infinitive, the commonest English-interference error. Index-scored MCQ.
